text.title: DDD Domain Events Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules grounding domain-event design + collection + outbox dispatch.

rule.statement: Domain Events MUST be immutable value records. In Python use `@dataclass(frozen=True)`; in C# use `record`; in Java/Kotlin use `record`/`data class` with final fields.

rationale.source: DDD Reference + Vaughn Vernon — Domain Events

rule.rationale: Mutable events allow handlers to change the payload mid-pipeline; downstream consumers receive divergent state.

rule.statement: Events MUST be raised inside aggregate command methods (`self._events.append(...)`), not in application services or domain services. The aggregate is the source of truth for what happened.
rule.rationale: Raising in services duplicates the policy across call sites and decouples event from the actual state change.

rule.statement: The application service MUST call `aggregate.collect_events()` ONLY after the DB transaction commits successfully. Events MUST NOT be dispatched before commit.
rule.rationale: Pre-commit dispatch leaks events for transactions that roll back; consumers act on state that never happened.

rule.statement: Every event MUST carry `event_id` (UUID), `occurred_at` (UTC timestamp), and an aggregate-identity field (e.g. `order_id`). `event_id` is used for idempotency by consumers.
rule.rationale: Without `event_id`, replays cause duplicate side-effects; without `occurred_at`, downstream ordering fails.

rule.statement: When publishing events to a message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SNS) the system MUST use the Outbox pattern: insert event row into `outbox` table inside the same DB transaction; a relay process picks up unprocessed rows and publishes them. Direct broker-publish in the application service is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Direct publish is a dual-write — DB commit + broker publish are not atomic. Brokers receive events for rolled-back transactions or miss events when the publish fails after commit.
